candidate_problems.text: How can 'public epistemic goods' and institutional legitimacy be operationalized and causally distinguished from adjacent factors like performance, coercion, cultural alignment, or communication quality? The conjecture is powerful but risks becoming unfalsifiable unless it specifies observable mechanisms, measures, and discriminating predictions about when legitimacy tracks organized self-correction rather than other sources of compliance or trust.

What institutional designs actually solve the collective-action problem of organized self-correction without creating new failure modes? The conjecture identifies concentrated costs and diffuse benefits, but leaves open which mechanisms—protected error-reporting, rotating review bodies, constitutionalized revision rules, insurance against blame, participatory feedback, external audits, etc.—reliably shift incentives, and under what conditions they instead produce bureaucracy, capture, performative transparency, or destabilizing overreaction.

What are the boundary conditions of the conjecture across institutional types and rates of change? It remains unresolved whether all institutions under rapid change need the same degree of visible adaptation, or whether some retain legitimacy through stability, tradition, or delegated expertise. A key open question is when consistency-preserving structures remain adaptive enough, and when demands for explicit self-revision themselves undermine legitimacy by signaling uncertainty, conflict, or weakness.
